description: F-LNG1 Zipf law at n=927 (S435) — direct scan, 2x growth since S345 baseline
hypothesis: Zipf α remains stable near canonical (≈0.97) at n=925 despite 2x lesson growth since S345
scan_scope: all .md/.json/.py/.txt/.sh in memory/lessons/, beliefs/, tasks/, domains/, tools/ (1540 files)

series.note: METHODOLOGY CORRECTION — scan replaces stale cache; S340-S344 cache artifacts now excluded
ARTIFACT — reverted to cache; exclude from series

alpha_decline.interpretation: Real decline — distribution flattening as corpus grows. At current rate, α would reach 0.7 at n≈1400.

structural_anomaly.finding: L-601 has 409 citations — 3.7x the #2 lesson (L-001 at 111). Super-Zipfian ratio at rank 1.
structural_anomaly.cause: Protocol crystallization: L-601 (structural enforcement theorem) embedded into tool implementations. Automated tool references inflate citation count beyond organic usage.
structural_anomaly.interpretation: Citation attractor effect. Protocol lessons embedded in tools create citation feedback loops not present in organic Zipf.
structural_anomaly.implication: Clean Zipf fit requires excluding tool-embedded citations or separating organic from structural citation channels.

f_lng2_note.critical_period_status: ACTIVE — organic corrections rare per F-LNG2 hypothesis
f_lng2_note.note: proxy-K below S345 floor (55813) due to compaction events. Still above 40k threshold.

f_dep1_note.interpretation: Orphan rate self-resolved from 72% to 4.3% without explicit dependency infrastructure. Citation practice created implicit dependency graph organically.

open_questions: Does α stabilize near 0.8 or continue declining toward 0.7 as n→1400?
Can tool-embedded citations be separated from organic citations to get cleaner Zipf fit?
Does L-601's super-Zipfian dominance represent a structural instability (all eggs in one basket) or healthy protocol crystallization?
